walkthroughs.title: OpenAI Manager Setup
walkthroughs.description: A complete beginner-friendly guide to setting up the OpenAI Manager for local model orchestration. Covers Node.js installation, cloning the repo, dependency setup, API key management via environment variables, model preference configuration, and launching the local dashboard.
steps: Install Node.js 18+ and npm from https://nodejs.org/
Run `npm install` inside the project folder

walkthroughs.title: Proot Sandbox Management
walkthroughs.description: Create and manage isolated Linux sandboxes with Proot for secure development on Android or low-privilege hosts. Covers install, login, toolchain setup, directory binds, and cleanup.
steps: Install tools: `apk add nodejs python3 git curl vim`
Exit with `exit`; remove with `proot-distro remove alpine`

walkthroughs.title: GGUF v3 Quantization: Q4_0 / Q4_K / Q5_K / Q8_0
walkthroughs.description: Deep dive into GGUF v3 quantization formats: block layouts, bit budgets, accuracy trade-offs, and exact commands to convert to FP16 then quantize to Q4_0, Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0. Includes perplexity benchmarking.
steps: Pick Q4_K_M for balance, Q8_0 for quality, Q4_0 for edge devices

walkthroughs.title: Install All Code-Assist CLIs in One Place
walkthroughs.description: One-shot setup for Slack CLI, JetBrains Toolbox, Aider, GitHub CLI, Ollama, ripgrep, fzf, and lazygit. Covers macOS, Linux, and Windows install paths plus useful shell aliases.
steps: Install a package manager (Homebrew / winget / apt / apk)
Slack CLI: `brew install slackcorp/slack/slack` or see Slack docs
GitHub CLI: `brew install gh` / `winget install GitHub.cli` / `apt install gh`
Productivity CLIs: `brew install ripgrep fzf lazygit jq`
Add aliases: `alias ai='aider'`, `alias lg='lazygit'`, `alias oll='ollama run llama3.1'`

walkthroughs.title: Aider + Ollama: Local AI Pair Programming
walkthroughs.description: Set up Aider to use a local Ollama model for private, offline AI pair programming. Covers Ollama install, pulling a coding model, launching Aider with the local endpoint, and everyday commands for asking questions, editing files, and committing changes.
steps: Pull a coding model: `ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:1.5b` (or `llama3.1`, `codellama`, `deepseek-coder-v2`)
Verify the model runs: `ollama run qwen2.5-coder:1.5b` — then type `/bye` to exit
Open your project repo in a terminal.
Add files to the chat: `/add src/myfile.py`
Ask for changes: `refactor this function to use async/await`
Review diffs, accept with `/commit`, or undo with `/undo`
Pro tip: add `export OLLAMA_API_BASE=http://localhost:11434` to your shell rc if Aider can't find Ollama.

about.title: Aegis Foundry — Living ASCII Dashboard
about.description: A sovereign, auto-updating terminal-style dashboard for tracking projects, mastering workflows, and surfacing the most important next steps. Built as a static GitHub Pages site with a Python data generator, rainbow ASCII art, and expandable walkthroughs.
features: Live ASCII headline renderer with rainbow hue cycling
GitHub project tracker with next-step focus
Curated technical walkthroughs (desktop + terminal + FAQ style)
Forum scaffold with localStorage post persistence

reviews.title: Claude
reviews.author: Chris Lloyd
reviews.summary: A careful, articulate reasoning model that excels at long-context writing and review.
reviews.verdict: Best for drafting, editing, and deep reasoning tasks.
reviews.body:
## Quick Take

Claude is my go-to model when I need a second brain for writing, reviewing, and structuring complex ideas. It is less of a doer and more of a thinker.

## In Depth

### What it does
Conversational AI with strong writing, coding, and reasoning capabilities.

### Where it shines
- Produces clean, human-readable drafts from messy notes.
- Great at spotting logical gaps in plans.

### Where it falls short
- Tool use varies by platform/plan.
- Sometimes over-explains or hedges.

### Pricing / Value
Competitive with other frontier models; value is high for content and code review.

### Who should use it
Writers, founders, and developers who need high-quality reasoning output.

## Rating Breakdown

- Usability: 4.5/5
- Value: 4.5/5
- Power: 4.5/5
- Sovereignty / Privacy: 3/5
- Overall: 4.6/5

reviews.title: Google Antigravity
reviews.author: Chris Lloyd
reviews.summary: An experimental Google AI feature aimed at reducing friction in workspace tasks.
reviews.verdict: Convenient for Google-centric users, but not ideal for sovereign workflows.
reviews.body:
## Quick Take

Google Antigravity is useful when I am already inside Google’s garden. For anything that needs to leave that garden, I prefer Kai.

## In Depth

### What it does
AI assistance embedded in Google Workspace apps.

### Where it shines
- Summarizing long email threads and documents.
- Generating quick replies.

### Where it falls short
- Data stays inside Google.
- Cannot orchestrate external repos, shell, or custom tools.

### Pricing / Value
Likely bundled with Google One / Workspace. Value depends on how deep you are in the ecosystem.

### Who should use it
Users who live in Gmail/Docs and want low-friction AI help.

## Rating Breakdown

- Usability: 4/5
- Value: 3/5
- Power: 3/5
- Sovereignty / Privacy: 2/5
- Overall: 3.5/5

reviews.title: Hermes
reviews.author: Chris Lloyd
reviews.summary: A capable open-weight model family that can run locally for privacy-sensitive work.
reviews.verdict: Best for privacy-first, offline, or self-hosted AI workflows.
reviews.body:
## Quick Take

Hermes represents the local-first option. When I do not want a request leaving my hardware, Hermes is the answer.

## In Depth

### What it does
Open-weight large language model runnable via Ollama, llama.cpp, or similar local runners.

### Where it shines
- Full privacy: no network required after download.
- Great for iterating on sensitive ideas without cloud exposure.

### Where it falls short
- Hardware demands scale quickly with model size.
- Not as polished as hosted APIs for complex tool chains.

### Pricing / Value
Free weights; cost is hardware and electricity. Excellent long-term value.

### Who should use it
Privacy-conscious users, homelab owners, and anyone building sovereign AI stacks.

## Rating Breakdown

- Usability: 3.5/5
- Value: 5/5
- Power: 4/5
- Sovereignty / Privacy: 5/5
- Overall: 4.3/5

reviews.title: Kai 9000
reviews.author: Chris Lloyd
reviews.summary: The personal AI endpoint that bridges plain English to real code, memory, and scheduled action.
reviews.verdict: Best for power users who want an always-on agent orchestrating their technical life.
reviews.body:
## Quick Take

Kai 9000 is the endpoint I use to turn rough ideas into commits, emails, scheduled tasks, and memory updates. It is not a chatbot — it is an operating layer that sits above my repos, inbox, and phone.

## In Depth

### What it does
Kai interprets plain-English commands, picks the right tools, executes shell / git / email / SMS actions, and remembers context across conversations.

### Where it shines
- Cross-tool orchestration: one sentence can trigger a repo change, an email, and a calendar event.
- Memory persistence: preferences, project state, and attention items survive across sessions.
- Scheduled autonomy: cron-like tasks run inside heartbeat loops.

### Where it falls short
- Requires a healthy sandbox / Termux connection for filesystem writes.
- Heavy use can hit provider rate limits.

### Pricing / Value
Currently bundled with my own infrastructure. Value is extremely high once the loop is closed.

### Who should use it
Builders who want an agent that actually *does* things, not just talks.

## Rating Breakdown

- Usability: 4/5
- Value: 5/5
- Power: 5/5
- Sovereignty / Privacy: 4/5
- Overall: 4.8/5

reviews.title: Local AI on a 2009 Xeon (No GPU, No AVX)
reviews.author: Chris Lloyd
reviews.summary: Real benchmark numbers from running a 45-model local LLM fleet on a 17-year-old Xeon X3430 — including a Q8 vs Q4 quantization shootout that upends the usual advice.
reviews.- \"Sustainable: hardware everyone else scrapped, kept genuinely useful
reviews.verdict: Wouldn't be a fun chat, but it works for us — measured, sustainable, and completely sovereign.
reviews.body:
## Quick Take

Everyone says you need a GPU, or at least a modern CPU with AVX-512, to run local AI. Our build server is a 2009 Xeon X3430 — four cores, 16GB ECC, spinning RAID, and NO AVX at all. It runs a 45-model ollama fleet around the clock and ships real, tested code. Here are the actual numbers, because almost nobody publishes benchmarks for hardware this old.

## In Depth

### What it does
CPU-only LLM inference (ollama / GGUF) on a machine old enough to have voted twice. 1-3B models handle routine decisions and drafts; a quantized 7B "senior" handles the hard asks asynchronously; deterministic code generators do the heavy lifting so the models only spend tokens where judgement is needed.

### The quantization shootout (the interesting numbers)
We ran llama3.2:1b in Q8_0 against its Q4_K_M sibling — 28 head-to-head rounds across 14 scored arenas (code, math, comparison, decisions, docs):
- Accuracy: statistically identical — matched pass-for-pass on 13 of 14 arenas, a single flip on the 14th.
- Speed: Q4_K_M was about 15% SLOWER warm (e.g. 14.8s vs 12.3s on short tasks). The internet's "Q4 is faster" advice assumes AVX2/AVX-512 SIMD kernels for K-quant dequantization. Without AVX, Q8_0's trivial dequant wins the compute race.
- RAM: Q4 wins the only prize that matters on 16GB — 807MB vs 1.3GB resident, a 38% headroom gain.
Ruling: on old CPUs, quantization is a RAM lever, not a speed lever. Measure your own box; don't import assumptions from GPU-land.

### More measured surprises
- Doubling inference threads from 2 to 4 physical cores gained only ~6% tokens/sec — the box is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound.
- A 7B coder model at ~5 tok/s is useless for chat but excellent as an async "slow court": give it minutes instead of seconds and it produces working, reviewable code that the small models can't.

### Where it falls short
Interactive conversation. Warm answers land in 12-25 seconds, cold loads in minutes. You would not want this as your chat companion — it's a factory floor, not a front desk.

### Pricing / Value
Hardware most people would recycle for free. Weights are free. The cost is electricity and patience — and the patience is the system's, not yours, if you design async.

### Who should use it
Homelabbers, sovereignty-minded builders, anyone with a retired server and a stubborn streak. Sustainability angle is real: this is e-waste doing production work.

## Rating Breakdown

- Usability: 3/5 (async only — chat is not the use case)
- Value: 5/5 (rescued hardware, zero recurring cost)
- Power: 4/5 (surprising ceiling with the right architecture)
- Sovereignty / Privacy: 5/5 (nothing ever leaves the box)
- Overall: 4.4/5

reviews.title: OpenClaw
reviews.author: Chris Lloyd
reviews.summary: A lightweight command-line AI assistant focused on quick local operations.
reviews.verdict: Ideal when you want AI help without opening a full agent dashboard.
reviews.body:
## Quick Take

OpenClaw is the scalpel to Kai's swiss-army-knife: small, fast, and terminal-native.

## In Depth

### What it does
Generates shell commands, explains CLI output, and helps script small utilities.

### Where it shines
- Speed. No UI, no setup, just pipe text in and get a command out.
- Good for learning new CLI tools.

### Where it falls short
- No built-in project memory or email/SMS integration.
- Best as a component, not a full system.

### Pricing / Value
Low cost / high utility for terminal-heavy workflows.

### Who should use it
Developers and DevOps people who live in the terminal.

## Rating Breakdown

- Usability: 4.5/5
- Value: 4/5
- Power: 3.5/5
- Sovereignty / Privacy: 4/5
- Overall: 4.2/5

title: Aegis Foundry Launchpad
tagline: The first page you open before writing any code.

seo.description: Terminal-first guides to install Claude Code CLI, Aider, Ollama, GGUF quantization, and every tool you actually need. Daily ASCII flex + foundry forums.

daily_flex.title: ASCII Sine-Wave of the Day
daily_flex.description: A deterministic sine wave rendered in ASCII, seeded by today's date. Refreshes once per day at 00:00 UTC.

workflow_cards.name: Claude Code CLI
workflow_cards.tagline: The most-used coding assistant in the terminal.
workflow_cards.why_use: Claude Code is Anthropic's official terminal agent. You describe what you want in plain English and it reads, edits, tests, and commits code from your shell. It is the fastest way to refactor, debug, scaffold, and explore a codebase without leaving the keyboard.

metrics.evidence: Highest mindshare among terminal-first AI coding tools; recommended by Aider/Cursor power-users for agentic shell workflows.

dependencies.name: Node.js 18+
dependencies.why: Claude Code is distributed as an npm package

dependencies.name: Git
dependencies.why: Claude Code uses git context for safer edits

dependencies.name: Anthropic API key
dependencies.why: Claude Code bills against your Anthropic account

install_methods.name: npm global — cross-platform
install_methods.pitfalls: If you see 'npm ERR! EACCES', configure a global npm prefix or use the npx fallback below.

install_methods.name: npx one-shot — no global install
steps: # Run the latest version without installing
install_methods.pitfalls: Slower startup because npx downloads/checks the package. Corporate proxies may block npx.

install_methods.name: Windows PowerShell + winget
pre_steps: # If Node is missing, install LTS:
# Refresh PATH in this session without restarting:
install_methods.pitfalls: PATH does not update until you open a new PowerShell window unless you refresh $env:Path manually.
install_methods.fallback: Download Node.js from https://nodejs.org/en/download, install, then npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

install_methods.name: WSL2 Ubuntu / Debian bash
pre_steps: # Install a modern Node if system node is old:
install_methods.pitfalls: Ubuntu's default nodejs package may be older than 18. Use the NodeSource curl pipe shown above if needed.
install_methods.fallback: curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.7/install.sh | bash && source ~/.bashrc && nvm install 20 && npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

install_methods.name: macOS Homebrew
install_methods.pitfalls: Homebrew on Apple Silicon installs to /opt/homebrew; make sure it is in your PATH.

dependency_install.name: Install Node.js LTS on Windows (no admin: use nvm-windows)

use_cases.title: Scaffold a feature from a blank terminal
use_cases.context: You just created an empty folder and want a working feature without touching an editor.
demo_commands: > Create a minimal Python Flask app with one route '/' that returns JSON {status: 'ok'}
# Claude will show planned files. Accept each with 'y'.
use_cases.expected_output: app.py appears, requirements are inferred, and the server starts on http://127.0.0.1:5000.

use_cases.title: Fix a failing test
use_cases.context: You ran the test suite and one test fails with a confusing error.
demo_commands: # Copy the error, then launch Claude Code in the project root:
> Test X failed with 'Cannot find module ./helpers'. Diagnose and fix.
# Review the diff Claude proposes, accept with 'y'.
use_cases.expected_output: The missing import or path is corrected and the test passes.

use_cases.title: Refactor a large file into modules
use_cases.context: src/app.js is 600 lines and you want it split into src/lib/ modules with tests.
demo_commands: > Refactor src/app.js into smaller modules under src/lib/ and add unit tests for the extracted helpers.
use_cases.expected_output: New files appear under src/lib/, src/app.js shrinks, and tests are added.

use_cases.title: Understand a foreign codebase
use_cases.context: You cloned a repo and need a 30-second orientation before contributing.
demo_commands: > What does this repo do, what are the main entry points, and how do I run it?
use_cases.expected_output: A concise summary of the project purpose, key files, and run commands.

pro_tips: Run Claude Code inside a git repo so every change is reversible with git checkout -- .
Type /compact when the conversation gets long; Claude will summarize context.
Type /cost to see estimated API usage for the session.
In CI/automation, restrict tools with claude-code --allowedTools edit,bash,view
Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in your shell profile or load it from a .env file.
